description: vLLM capacity drill-down: platform KPIs → per-model saturation signals and latency SLOs → per-replica load balance. Filter: namespace → model → pod.

panels.description: Total generation token throughput across all selected models. Primary output capacity metric — size the fleet to sustain this at SLO latency.
panels.title: Gen Tokens/s

panels.description: Requests currently being decoded on GPU. Stable value = healthy concurrency. Sudden drop = possible engine crash or restart.
panels.title: Requests Running

panels.description: Requests queued waiting for a GPU slot (KV-cache block availability). Any sustained non-zero value = capacity exhausted. Waiting > 0 with low GPU utilisation = KV-cache bottleneck, not compute.
panels.title: Requests Waiting

panels.description: Average GPU KV-cache utilisation across pods. Above 85% = preemption risk. The primary memory capacity constraint — when this saturates the engine queues or drops requests.
panels.title: KV Cache %

panels.description: Rate of KV-cache evictions. Non-zero = GPU memory was full and the scheduler evicted requests. Any sustained value here means you are running over memory capacity.
panels.title: Preemptions/min

panels.description: Successfully completed requests per second. Divide Gen Tokens/s by Request Rate to get average output length. Dropping rate with stable running count = longer responses.
panels.title: Request Rate
Saturation Signals — Queue Depth & KV Cache Pressure

panels.description: Running (actively using GPU) and waiting (blocked on KV-cache) request counts per model. Waiting > 0 sustained = capacity exhausted. Healthy: running high, waiting zero. KV-cache bound: KV cache at 85%+ while waiting grows. Compute bound: GPU at 100%, waiting near zero.
panels.title: Request Queue Depth by Model

panels.description: Average GPU KV-cache utilisation per model. 85% threshold line marks the preemption boundary — crossing it means the engine must evict requests. Sustained proximity = need more GPU VRAM per pod, or reduce max concurrent context length.
panels.title: GPU KV Cache % by Model

panels.description: Generation and prompt token throughput per model. Gen tok/s is the primary output capacity metric. Prompt rate >> gen rate = short-answer workload with expensive prefill. Watch for divergence between models to spot hot spots.
panels.title: Token Rate by Model

panels.description: Successfully completed requests per second per model. Divide gen tok/s by req/s to get average output length. A falling req/s with stable gen tok/s means responses are getting longer.
panels.title: Request Throughput by Model

panels.description: Median end-to-end request latency (queue + prefill + decode). Typical user experience baseline.
panels.title: E2E P50

panels.description: 99th percentile end-to-end latency — worst-case user experience. Should stay under your SLO ceiling.
panels.title: E2E P99

panels.description: Median time to first token (prefill phase latency). User perceives this as the delay before the response starts appearing.
panels.title: TTFT P50

panels.description: 99th percentile TTFT. Spikes indicate compute saturation during prefill: very long prompts, high concurrency, or GPU at capacity.
panels.title: TTFT P99

panels.description: Median time per output token (inter-token decode latency). Determines streaming speed perceived by users. Lower = faster streaming.
panels.title: TPOT P50

panels.description: 99th percentile TPOT. Rising P99 = decode is becoming a bottleneck; fewer parallel sequences can be sustained. Correlate with Requests Running.
panels.title: TPOT P99

panels.description: End-to-end latency P50 and P99 by model over time. Growing P99 with stable TTFT = decode bottleneck. Both growing together = prefill/compute saturation or queue build-up.
panels.title: E2E Request Latency

panels.description: TTFT P50 and P99 by model — prefill phase latency. Spikes = heavy prompts or GPU compute saturation during prefill. Sustained elevation = need more compute or shorter context limits.
panels.title: Time to First Token (TTFT)

panels.description: TPOT P50 and P99 by model — decode phase latency per token. Rising TPOT = more concurrent sequences or longer sequences → higher memory pressure per decode step. Correlate with KV cache %.
panels.title: Time per Output Token (TPOT)

panels.description: Fraction of KV-cache block lookups served from the prefix cache per model. High rate = significant compute savings on repeated prefixes (system prompts, few-shot examples). Zero = no prefix reuse — consider whether workloads have shared prompt prefixes. Requires prefix caching enabled in vLLM config.
panels.title: Prefix Cache Hit Rate by Model

panels.description: KV-cache eviction rate per second per model. Non-zero = GPU memory was full and the scheduler had to pause requests. Sustained preemptions = reduce concurrent context length, lower max model concurrency, or add GPU memory.
panels.title: Preemption Rate by Model
Per-Pod Detail — Replica Health & Load Balance

panels.description: GPU KV-cache utilisation per replica. A single pod above 85% will preempt requests even if the model-level average looks fine. Skew between pods = uneven load distribution or a replica-specific memory leak.
panels.title: KV Cache % per Pod

panels.description: Running and waiting request counts per replica. A pod with persistent waiting while peers are idle = load-balancer skew or readiness issue on that replica. All pods waiting together = global capacity exhaustion.
panels.title: Requests per Pod

panels.description: Output token throughput per replica. Uneven rates = uneven work distribution (check load-balancer routing). All pods low with users active = upstream throttling or the model is producing very short outputs.
panels.title: Generation Token Rate per Pod

title: vLLM Inference Capacity
